en.extract:
Archbold's nightjar (Eurostopodus archboldi), also known as the mountain eared-nightjar or cloud-forest nightjar, is a dark and densely spotted species of nightjar in the family Caprimulgidae. It is endemic to New Guinea and is the only nightjar species inhabiting the high-elevation subtropical and tropical montane forests of New Guinea. This bird is named after the American explorer Richard Archbold, whose expedition in New Guinea in 1933-1934 led to the discovery of the species.
Archbold's nightjar is a nocturnal species, roosting on the ground during the day and emerging to forage at night. E. archboldi is a proficient hunter, specialized in catching large insects while in flight.
Due to its restricted geographic range and secretive nature, it is not commonly encountered, and very little is understood about this species. Additionally, due to the great diversity of other avian species in New Guinea, there has been a lack of targeted studies on nightjar species, particularly E. archboldi.


Taxonomy
Archbold's nightjar is classified in the order Caprimulgiformes, and the nightjar family Caprimulgidae under the genus Eurostopodus alongside six other bird species. Prior to its reclassification into the genus Eurostopodus, Archbold's nightjar was formally placed under the genus Lyncornis, with its type locality recorded as the West slope of Mount Tafa at an altitude of 2,400 metres (7,900 ft), New Guinea. Although no formal publication documents this reclassification, the earliest known reference that cites Archbold's nightjar as Eurostopodus archboldi appears in Results of the Archbold Expeditions. No. 43. (1942). As such, all subsequent literature published after 1942 refers to the species as Eurostopodus archboldi. Archbold's nightjar is monotypic, lacking any recognized subspecies.
Its closest relative, the Papuan nightjar (Eurostopodus papuensis), also inhabits the forests of New Guinea and looks morphologically similar to Archbold's nightjar, but it is smaller and darker with less spots, and occurs in lower altitudes.


Etymology
The genus name Eurostopodus is derived from the Ancient Greek words eurōstos, meaning "strong" or "stout" and podos, meaning "foot", while the species name archboldi honors the American explorer, Richard Archbold, who conducted a series of expeditions in New Guinea which led to the discovery of the great biodiversity of this region. During his first expedition in southeast New Guinea, known as the First Archbold Expedition (1933-1934), Archbold collected a total of 3,200 bird specimens, including E. archboldi.


Description
The body length of adults is around 26–30 centimetres (10–12 in). Adult wingspan is ~19.6–21.9 centimetres (7.7–8.6 in); tail length is ~13.4–15.7 centimetres (5.3–6.2 in); the bill length is ~1.35–1.61 centimetres (0.53–0.63 in). Archbold's nightjar's small, hooked beak is black, its irises are dark brown, and its legs and feet are dark grey or brown with black claws.


Plumage
The top of E. archboldi's head is dark brown and streaked buff-rufous along the edges of the feathers. Sides of the crown are grey-brown with thin black streaks. The lore and ear coverts are black-dark brown and lightly spotted buff. E. archboldi's dark brown throat is vividly covered in buff-rufous colored spots, with a distinct white patch on both sides of the lower throat. The mantle and back are black-dark brown with vivid buff-rufous colored spots and grey streaks. This blackish-brown color transitions into a slightly paler, greyish color on the rump and the uppertail coverts. The breast is dark brown with bold, short buff-rufous streaks, and feathers become increasingly spotted buff-white as we reach the belly down to the undertail coverts. At rest, the dark brown tail is held into a rigid "tent" shape. The innermost tail feathers are faintly barred grey-white, whereas the outer tail feathers have a distinct pale grey-white bar and a broad buff-tawny bar on the edges of the feathers. Wing coverts are black-dark brown and spotted rufous, buff and tawny, with greater spotting on lesser coverts. Primaries and secondaries are black-dark brown, but secondaries are spotted rufous-tawny on the edges of the feathers. Tertials are also dark-brown, but mottled greyish-brown in addition to the buff spots.
Male and female individuals closely resemble each other, with some field guides describing females featuring more cinnamon-tawny spots on their dorsal and breast feathers, and thin brownish-yellow tips on the tail feathers instead of the grey-white tips found in males. Juvenile plumage differs from the adult plumage. Juveniles are entirely ashy grey-white, and possess a dark eye-patch. Their upperparts are lightly spotted black and their underparts are barred dark grey, interspersed with a few rufous feathers.


Distribution and habitat
Archbold's nightjar is a species endemic to New Guinea, dwelling in the highlands of the Central Cordillera mountain ranges. It has a patchy distribution, restricted to montane forests situated near clearings and open heathy regions. In fact, Archbold's nightjar is the sole high-mountain nightjar species of New Guinea, found in elevations of 1,800 metres (5,900 ft) up to 3,260 metres (10,700 ft). Records indicate that this species is sedentary , exhibiting limited seasonal movement.


Behaviour
This secretive bird is nocturnal and crepuscular, emerging at night and exhibiting peak activity at dusk when it forages. During the day, individuals typically roost on the ground, on logs, or on branches. These roosting sites are generally found in small forest clearings. E. archboldi may occur solitarily, in pairs, or even in small groups. In fact, in some instances, 2-3 individuals may roost together, resting alongside one another across adjacent branches.


Flight
Eurostopodus archboldi has an undulating, fluttery flight pattern consisting of rapid wing strokes interspersed with long sweeping glides. In flight, the wings appear elongated with blunt-ended, "fingered" wing tips, an adaptation consistent with their skillful gliding maneuverability. Like other nightjar species, Archbold's nightjar holds its wings upward in a "V" shape as it glides.


Diet
Like other members of the Caprimulgidae family, Archbold's nightjar is exclusively an insectivore. At dusk, individuals have been reported to feed by sallying, catching insects in flight before promptly returning to perch on a nearby branch or log. Stomach content analysis of captured specimens has documented that E. archboldi prefers feeding on larger insect species. Commonly consumed insects include beetles, moths, and cicadas. E. archboldi are agile predators, flying swiftly and hunting over grassy terrains or in and around forest clearings, flying low at ~30 feet (9.1 m) above the ground, but some have also been observed to sally amongst the treetops.


Reproduction
Across E. archboldi's range, the breeding season occurs between October to early December with an estimated generation time of 4.56 years. Archbold's nightjars do not build nests. A clutch consists of a single egg, which is laid directly on the ground on top of leaf-litter. Nest sites are typically located amongst stones and shrubs, and may be concealed from the surrounding vegetation, allowing individuals to blend in with their environment because of their cryptic coloration. Likes roost sites, nest sites are usually situated in forest clearings or forest edges. Eggs are white, and unlike other nightjar species, lack blotches.


Vocalizations
Not much is known of E. archboldi's vocalizations, as it is a relatively quiet bird. Its song is mainly heard at dusk, and consists of a soft guttural call of 2-3 short trilled tchrrrt notes.
If a threat is detected and an adult is startled from its nest, it may produce a very loud alarm call before retreating to a nearby branch. While perched, it will remain motionless until the intruder leaves and might produce a liquid trill consisting of soft whur-whur-whur notes. Alternatively, the individual might engage in displays of aggression consisting fluttering movements as a means of distracting the intruder. If the threat does not leave the nest site, adults may fly back and attempt to deter the intruder by hovering over them and emitting a harsh hissing alarm call.


Conservation
The IUCN red list currently ranks Archbold's nightjar as "Least Concern". Although the species is known to occupy a geographic range which encompasses large tracts of undisturbed forest, including conservation sites over a portion of its range, its overall population trend is thought to be decreasing. Despite the population size not being formally assessed, its restricted geographic range exhibits E. archboldi's strong reliance on montane forest habitat. As such, given the reduction in tree cover driven by deforestation within its range over the past decade, it is speculated that this rate of habitat loss has likely led to an estimated population decline of 5% during this period.
Despite indications of population decline and threats to Archbold's nightjar, there are no clear conservation decisions implemented as of yet because knowledge of this elusive species remains limited. Moreover, the lack of targeted research and monitoring programs for this species further limits understanding of its ecology, population dynamics, and potential conservation needs.


References


External links
Explore Species: Archbold's Nightjar at eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Archbold's nightjar photo gallery on Flickr

'''Archbold's nightjar (''Eurostopodus archboldi'')''', also known as the '''mountain eared-nightjar''' or '''cloud-forest nightjar''', is a dark and densely spotted species of [[nightjar]] in the family [[Nightjar|Caprimulgidae]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":7" /> It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[New Guinea]] and is the only nightjar species inhabiting the high-elevation subtropical and tropical [[montane forests]] of New Guinea. This bird is named after the American explorer [[Richard Archbold]], whose expedition in New Guinea in 1933-1934 led to the discovery of the species.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" />

Archbold's nightjar is a [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] species, [[roosting]] on the ground during the day and emerging to forage at night. ''E. archboldi'' is a proficient hunter, specialized in catching large insects while in flight.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Due to its restricted geographic range and secretive nature, it is not commonly encountered, and very little is understood about this species. Additionally, due to the great diversity of other avian species in New Guinea, there has been a lack of targeted studies on nightjar species, particularly ''E. archboldi''.<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal |last1=Diamond |first1=Jared |last2=Bishop |first2=K. David |date=2023 |title=What's so special about New Guinea birds?1 |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume=143 |issue=2 |doi=10.25226/bboc.v143i2.2023.a6 |issn=0007-1595|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Paliwal |first1=G.T. |last2=Bhandarkar |first2=S.V. |date=2020 |title=Observations on habitat use and breeding biology of Indian nightjar: Caprimulgus asiaticus (Lathum, 1790): Caprimulgidae |url=https://www.irjes.com/Papers/vol9-issue5/B09050609.pdf |journal=International Refereed Journal of Engineering and Science |volume=9 |issue=5}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==

Archbold's nightjar is classified in the order [[Caprimulgiformes]], and the nightjar family [[Nightjar|Caprimulgidae]] under the genus [[Eurostopodus]] alongside six other bird species.<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2011-10-08 |title=Rediscovery of Heinrich's Nightjar Eurostopodus diabolicus |url=http://kukila.org/index.php/KKL/article/view/195/193 |journal=KUKILA |language=en |volume=9 |issn=0216-9223 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191015011107/http://kukila.org/index.php/KKL/article/view/195/193 |archive-date=2019-10-15}}</ref> Prior to its reclassification into the genus ''[[Eurostopodus]]'', Archbold's nightjar was formally placed under the genus ''[[Lyncornis]]'', with its [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] recorded as the West slope of Mount Tafa at an altitude of {{Convert|2400|m|ft}}, [[New Guinea]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Mayr |first1=Ernst |last2=Rand |first2=Austin L. |date=1935 |title=Results of the Archbold expeditions. No. 6: Twenty-four apparently undescribed birds from New Guinea and the D'Entrecasteaux Archipelago |url=http://hdl.handle.net/2246/4098 |journal=American Museum Novitates |issue=814 |hdl=2246/4098 }}</ref> Although no formal publication documents this reclassification, the earliest known reference that cites Archbold's nightjar as ''Eurostopodus archboldi'' appears in ''Results of the Archbold Expeditions. No. 43.'' (1942).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Rand |first=Austin L. |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstreams/332aba43-1bd3-4047-8238-c51422955b2a/download |title=Results of the Archbold expeditions. No 43: Birds of the 1938-1939 New Guinea expedition |publisher=The American Museum of Natural History |year=1942}}</ref> As such, all subsequent literature published after 1942 refers to the species as ''Eurostopodus archboldi.'' Archbold's nightjar is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]], lacking any recognized subspecies.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Its closest relative, the [[Papuan nightjar|Papuan nightjar (''Eurostopodus papuensis'')]], also inhabits the forests of New Guinea and looks morphologically similar to Archbold's nightjar, but it is smaller and darker with less spots, and occurs in lower altitudes.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=McCullough |first1=Jenna |last2=DeCicco |first2=Lucas |last3=Boseto |first3=David |last4=Moyle |first4=Robert |last5=Andersen |first5=Michael |last6=McCullough |first6=Jenna M. |last7=DeCicco |first7=Lucas H. |last8=Boseto |first8=David |last9=Moyle |first9=Robert G. |last10=Andersen |first10=Michael J. |date=2025 |title=What Is an Eared Nightjar? Ultraconserved Elements Clarify the Evolutionary Relationships of Eurostopodus and Lyncornis Nightjars (Aves: Caprimulgidae) |url=https://ssbbulletin.org/article/id/4472/ |journal=Bulletin of the Society of Systematic Biologists |language= |volume=4 |issue=1 |doi=10.18061/bssb.v4i1.10183 |issn=|doi-access=free }}</ref>

=== Etymology ===
The genus name ''[[Eurostopodus]]'' is derived from the Ancient Greek words ''eurōstos'', meaning "strong" or "stout" and ''podos,'' meaning "foot", while the species name ''archboldi'' honors the American explorer, [[Richard Archbold]], who conducted a series of expeditions in New Guinea which led to the discovery of the great biodiversity of this region.<ref name=":4" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Key to Scientific Names - Birds of the World |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Eurostopodus |access-date=2025-11-04 |website=birdsoftheworld.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":5" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=The Auk |date=1977 |title=Obituary |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/auk/vol94/iss1/16 |journal=The Auk |volume=94 |issue=1}}</ref> During his first expedition in southeast New Guinea, known as the First Archbold Expedition (1933-1934), Archbold collected a total of 3,200 bird specimens, including ''E. archboldi''.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" />

== Description ==

The body length of adults is around {{Convert|26-30|cm|in}}.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> Adult wingspan is ~{{Convert|19.6-21.9|cm|in}}; tail length is ~{{Convert|13.4-15.7|cm|in}}; the bill length is ~{{Convert|1.35-1.61|cm|in}}.<ref name=":0" /> Archbold's nightjar's small, hooked beak is black, its irises are dark brown, and its legs and feet are dark grey or brown with black claws.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

=== Plumage ===
The top of ''E. archboldi''<nowiki/>'s head is dark brown and streaked [[Buff (colour)|buff]]-[[rufous]] along the edges of the feathers.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Sides of the crown are grey-brown with thin black streaks. The [[Lore (anatomy)|lore]] and [[ear covert]]s are black-dark brown and lightly spotted [[Buff (colour)|buff]]. ''E. archboldi's'' dark brown throat is vividly covered in buff-rufous colored spots, with a distinct white patch on both sides of the lower throat. The [[Mantle (bird anatomy)|mantle]] and back are black-dark brown with vivid buff-rufous colored spots and grey streaks. This blackish-brown color transitions into a slightly paler, greyish color on the [[Bird anatomy|rump]] and the [[uppertail covert]]s. The breast is dark brown with bold, short buff-rufous streaks, and feathers become increasingly spotted buff-white as we reach the belly down to the [[undertail coverts]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Pratt |first1=Thane K. |title=Birds of New Guinea |last2=Beehler |first2=Bruce M. |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |edition=2nd}}</ref> At rest, the dark brown tail is held into a rigid "tent" shape.<ref name=":0" /> The innermost tail feathers are faintly barred grey-white, whereas the outer tail feathers have a distinct pale grey-white bar and a broad buff-tawny bar on the edges of the feathers. [[Wing coverts]] are black-dark brown and spotted rufous, buff and [[Tawny (color)|tawny]], with greater spotting on lesser coverts.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> [[Primaries (birds)|Primaries]] and [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]] are black-dark brown, but secondaries are spotted rufous-tawny on the edges of the feathers.<ref name=":0" /> [[Tertial feather|Tertials]] are also dark-brown, but mottled greyish-brown in addition to the buff spots.

Male and female individuals closely resemble each other, with some field guides describing females featuring more cinnamon-tawny spots on their dorsal and breast feathers, and thin brownish-yellow tips on the tail feathers instead of the grey-white tips found in males.<ref name=":0" /> Juvenile plumage differs from the adult plumage.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> Juveniles are entirely ashy grey-white, and possess a dark eye-patch. Their upperparts are lightly spotted black and their underparts are barred dark grey, interspersed with a few rufous feathers.

== Distribution and habitat ==
Archbold's nightjar is a species endemic to New Guinea, dwelling in the highlands of the [[New Guinea Highlands|Central Cordillera mountain ranges]].<ref name=":6" /> It has a patchy distribution, restricted to [[montane forests]] situated near clearings and open heathy regions.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> In fact, Archbold's nightjar is the sole high-mountain nightjar species of New Guinea, found in elevations of {{Convert|1800|m|ft}} up to {{Convert|3260|m|ft}}.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite book |last=Cleere |first=N |title=Handbook of the birds of the world: Barn-owls to hummingbirds |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=1999 |editor-last=del Hoyo |editor-first=J |volume=5 |location=Barcelona |editor-last2=Elliot |editor-first2=A |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=J}}</ref> Records indicate that this species is [[Resident bird|sedentary]] , exhibiting limited seasonal movement.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

== Behaviour ==
This secretive bird is [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] and [[Crepuscular animal|crepuscular]], emerging at night and exhibiting peak activity at dusk when it forages.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":7" /> During the day, individuals typically roost on the ground, on logs, or on branches.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> These roosting sites are generally found in small [[forest clearing]]s. ''E. archboldi'' may occur solitarily, in pairs, or even in small groups.<ref name=":0" /> In fact, in some instances, 2-3 individuals may roost together, resting alongside one another across adjacent branches.

=== Flight ===

''Eurostopodus archboldi'' has an undulating, fluttery flight pattern consisting of rapid wing strokes interspersed with long sweeping glides.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> In flight, the wings appear elongated with blunt-ended, "fingered" wing tips, an adaptation consistent with their skillful gliding maneuverability.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Citation |last=Bildstein |first=Keith L. |title=Raptor Migration |date=2018 |work=Birds of Prey: Biology and conservation in the XXI century |pages=123–138 |editor-last=Sarasola |editor-first=José Hernán |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-73745-4_5 |access-date=2025-11-07 |place=Cham |publisher=Springer International Publishing |language=en |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-73745-4_5 |isbn=978-3-319-73745-4 |editor2-last=Grande |editor2-first=Juan Manuel |editor3-last=Negro |editor3-first=Juan José|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite thesis |last=Bachmann |first=Thomas Willem |title=Anatomical, morphometrical and biomechanical studies of barn owls' and pigeons' wings |date=2010 |publisher=Publikationsserver der RWTH Aachen University |url=https://publications.rwth-aachen.de/record/51750 |place=Aachen |language=English}}</ref> Like other nightjar species, Archbold's nightjar holds its wings upward in a "V" shape as it glides.<ref name=":2" />

=== Diet ===
Like other members of the [[Caprimulgidae]] family, Archbold's nightjar is exclusively an [[insectivore]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Cleere |first=Nigel |title=Nightjars: A guide to the nightjars, nighthawks, and their relatives |publisher=Yale University Press |year=1998 |isbn= |location=United States}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Hufford |first=Linda |title=Hand-Rearing Birds |year=2020 |editor-last=Duerr |editor-first=Rebecca S. |edition=2nd |chapter=Goatsuckers |pages=497–508 |doi=10.1002/9781119167792.ch32 |isbn=978-1-119-16775-4 |editor-last2=Gage |editor-first2=Laurie J.}}</ref> At dusk, individuals have been reported to feed by [[Hawking (birds)|sallying]], catching insects in flight before promptly returning to perch on a nearby branch or log.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Holyoak |first=D.T. |title=Nightjars and Their Allies: The Caprimulgiformes |publisher=Nature |year=2001}}</ref> Stomach content analysis of captured specimens has documented that ''E. archboldi'' prefers feeding on larger insect species.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":7" /> Commonly consumed insects include [[beetle]]s, [[moth]]s, and [[cicada]]s. ''E. archboldi'' are agile predators, flying swiftly and hunting over grassy terrains or in and around forest clearings, flying low at ~{{Convert|30|feet|m}} above the ground, but some have also been observed to sally amongst the treetops.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last=Diamond |first=Jared M. |title=Avifauna of the eastern highlands of New Guinea |publisher=Cambridge, Mass |year=1972}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
Across ''E. archboldi''<nowiki/>'s range, the [[breeding season]] occurs between October to early December with an estimated [[generation time]] of 4.56 years.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":8" /> Archbold's nightjars do not build nests. A [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] consists of a single egg, which is laid directly on the ground on top of leaf-litter.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> Nest sites are typically located amongst stones and shrubs, and may be concealed from the surrounding vegetation, allowing individuals to blend in with their environment because of their [[cryptic coloration]]. Likes roost sites, nest sites are usually situated in forest clearings or forest edges.<ref name=":1" /> Eggs are white, and unlike other [[nightjar]] species, lack blotches.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Gibbs |first=David |title=Mountain eared nightjar in Arfak Mountains, Irian Jaya: Range extension and first description of nest and egg. |url=https://www.dutchbirding.nl/journal/ref/DB_18:246-247 |journal=Dutch Birding |volume=18 |pages=246–247}}</ref>

=== Vocalizations ===
Not much is known of ''E. archboldi'''s vocalizations, as it is a relatively quiet bird.<ref name=":2" /> Its song is mainly heard at dusk, and consists of a soft guttural call of 2-3 short trilled ''tchrrrt'' notes.<ref name=":0" />

If a threat is detected and an adult is startled from its nest, it may produce a very loud [[alarm call]] before retreating to a nearby branch.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":7" /> While perched, it will remain motionless until the intruder leaves and might produce a liquid trill consisting of soft ''whur-whur-whur'' notes.<ref name=":0" /> Alternatively, the individual might engage in displays of aggression consisting fluttering movements as a means of distracting the intruder. If the threat does not leave the nest site, adults may fly back and attempt to deter the intruder by hovering over them and emitting a harsh hissing alarm call.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" />

== Conservation ==
The [[IUCN Red List|IUCN red list]] currently ranks Archbold's nightjar as "[[Least-concern species|Least Concern]]".<ref name=":8" /> Although the species is known to occupy a geographic range which encompasses large tracts of undisturbed forest, including conservation sites over a portion of its range, its overall population trend is thought to be decreasing.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":8">{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22689680/264111164 |title=Eurostopodus archboldi: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2024: e.T22689680A264111164 |last=IUCN |date=2024 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2024-2.rlts.t22689680a264111164.en |language=en}}</ref> Despite the population size not being formally assessed, its restricted geographic range exhibits ''E. archbold''i's strong reliance on montane forest habitat.<ref name=":8" /> As such, given the reduction in tree cover driven by [[deforestation]] within its range over the past decade, it is speculated that this rate of habitat loss has likely led to an estimated population decline of 5% during this period.<ref name=":8" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Vizzuality |title=Forest Monitoring, Land Use & Deforestation Trends {{!}} Global Forest Watch |url=https://www.globalforestwatch.org/ |access-date=2025-11-07 |website=www.globalforestwatch.org |language=en}}</ref>

Despite indications of population decline and threats to Archbold's nightjar, there are no clear conservation decisions implemented as of yet because knowledge of this elusive species remains limited.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":8" /> Moreover, the lack of targeted research and monitoring programs for this species further limits understanding of its ecology, [[population dynamics]], and potential conservation needs.<ref name=":9" /><ref name=":8" />
